title: Java Fundamentals: Architecture & Core Basics
description: The definitive masterclass on Von Neumann hardware execution, the Java Virtual Machine, the complete primitive data type families, arithmetic casting, and the Math computation class.

subtopics.title: The Von Neumann Architecture
subtopics.content:
Before writing code, you must understand the machine executing it. Most modern computers are built on the Von Neumann architecture, where both your program's instructions and its data are stored in the exact same memory space [cite: 159, 160].

[Diagram of Von Neumann Architecture showing CPU, ALU, Control Unit, Memory, and I/O Devices]

The CPU (Central Processing Unit) acts as the brain, fetching instructions from Memory, decoding them, and executing them sequentially[cite: 174, 177]. Because instructions and data share the same pathway (the bus), the CPU often has to wait, creating the 'Von Neumann Bottleneck'[cite: 201, 211, 213].
subtopics.callout_text: To bypass the bottleneck, hardware relies on 'Cache' (lightning-fast memory inside the CPU) and 'Buffers' (temporary staging areas for data moving through I/O ports)[cite: 2, 214].
list_items: **PCs & Laptops:** Use shared memory for multitasking software[cite: 218].
**Mobile Platforms:** ARM-based smartphones use this architecture for extreme power efficiency[cite: 220].
**Embedded Systems:** Microcontrollers in cars and IoT devices[cite: 222].

subtopics.title: Compilers vs. Interpreters
subtopics.content:
Computers only understand machine code (1s and 0s)[cite: 155]. High-level languages must be translated. A **Compiler** reads your entire program and creates a permanent machine-code file[cite: 156]. An **Interpreter** reads and executes your code line-by-line, on the fly[cite: 156].

Java uses a two-step process to achieve its 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' capability. First, the Java Compiler translates your code into an intermediate language called **Bytecode**. Then, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) acts as an interpreter, translating that Bytecode into the specific machine code required by your OS.
subtopics.callout_text: Developers use IDEs (Integrated Development Environments) to automate compiling. Interpreters and the JVM are the backbone of Cloud Computing, allowing code to be sent to massive servers (like AWS) and executed dynamically without being pre-compiled for that server's specific hardware[cite: 2, 224].
list_items: **Source Code (`.java`):** The high-level language you type.
**Bytecode (`.class`):** The imaginary machine code generated by the compiler.
**Machine Code:** What the JVM generates for the physical CPU[cite: 155, 156].

subtopics.title: Program Structure & Output
subtopics.content:
Java has a rigid structure. All code must live inside a Method, and every Method must live inside a Class. To communicate with the outside world, we use output statements.

`System.out.print` pushes text to the monitor and leaves the cursor on the same line. `System.out.println` pushes the text and then forces the cursor to a new line.
subtopics.callout_text: The compiler ignores comments. Use `//` for single-line notes, and `/* ... */` for multi-line block comments to explain complex logic.
list_items: Java is strictly case-sensitive.
Every basic operational statement must end with a semicolon `;`.

subtopics.title: The 3 Types of Programming Errors
subtopics.content:
Testing and debugging is a critical part of the software life cycle[cite: 109, 111]. When your program breaks, it will fall into one of three distinct error categories.

[Diagram of the 3 Types of Programming Errors: Syntax, Logical, Runtime]
subtopics.callout_text: Logical errors are the most dangerous because the compiler will NOT warn you[cite: 117]. The program will run perfectly, but it will silently generate the wrong data.
list_items: **Syntax Error:** Breaking the grammar rules of the language. The code will mathematically refuse to compile[cite: 114, 115].
**Logical Error:** Implementing the wrong logic. Detected only by testing the output[cite: 117, 118].
**Runtime Error:** An impossible operation (like dividing by zero) that causes an unusual and fatal termination while the app is actively running[cite: 119, 120].

subtopics.title: The Integer Family
subtopics.content: A variable is a named memory location capable of storing data[cite: 239]. Because RAM is limited, Java offers four different sizes of boxes just for storing whole numbers. Choosing the right box optimizes performance.
subtopics.callout_text: If you type a whole number in Java, the compiler automatically assumes it is an `int`. If you need to store it in a `long` box, you MUST append a capital `L` to the end of the number so the compiler builds the larger 64-bit box!
list_items: `int`: -2.1 Billion to 2.1 Billion
`long`: -9 Quintillion to 9 Quintillion

subtopics.title: The Floating-Point Family
subtopics.content: To store real numbers (decimals) or extremely massive scientific numbers, Java provides the floating-point family[cite: 241, 244].
subtopics.callout_text: Just like integers default to `int`, any decimal typed in Java automatically defaults to a `double`. If you try to put a decimal in a `float` box without appending an 'f', the code will crash!
list_items: `float`: Supports up to 3.4 x 10^38.
`double`: Supports up to 1.7 x 10^308.

subtopics.title: Identifiers, Keywords & Constants
subtopics.content:
An 'Identifier' is the name you give your variable. You must avoid reserved Java keywords (like `public` or `class`), and you cannot start a name with a number. By convention, variables use `camelCase` [cite: 243].

If you want to maintain a value in a named location that cannot be changed (like the value of PI), you use the `final` keyword to declare a named constant[cite: 265].
subtopics.callout_text: Constants prevent accidental logic bugs where a core mathematical rule is accidentally overwritten deep inside a program[cite: 265].
list_items: Variables = `camelCase`.
Classes = `PascalCase`.
Constants = `UPPER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES`.

subtopics.title: Arithmetic & Integer Division
subtopics.content: An arithmetic expression is a set of symbols that represents a numeric value[cite: 244]. Java supports addition `+`, subtraction `-`, multiplication `*`, division `/`, and modulus `%`[cite: 244, 251].
subtopics.callout_text: To correctly get a decimal answer when dividing, at least one of the numbers must be a floating-point number. `9.0 / 4` will correctly evaluate to `2.25`[cite: 247, 255].
list_items: Integer / Integer = Integer (Decimal is lost).
Use `%` to find remainders or identify even/odd numbers[cite: 251].

subtopics.title: Compound Assignment Operators
subtopics.content: Updating a variable based on its own current value (e.g., `x = x + 1;`) is extremely common. Java provides shorthand Compound Operators to perform the math and the assignment simultaneously[cite: 263].
subtopics.callout_text: These operators evaluate the math on the right, and instantly assign the resulting value back into the variable on the left, erasing the old value[cite: 263].
list_items: `X += 1;` is exactly the same as `X = X + 1;`
`X *= 5;` is exactly the same as `X = X * 5;`

subtopics.title: Implicit vs. Explicit Type Casting
subtopics.content: A mixed-type expression involves operands of different data types[cite: 255]. Java must figure out how to evaluate them. It uses **Implicit Promotion** to automatically upgrade smaller data types to larger ones. If you need to force a downgrade, you use **Explicit Casting**.
subtopics.callout_text: Be extremely careful with parentheses when casting! `(double) (y / x)` will do the integer division first (yielding 2), and then cast the destroyed number into `2.0`. You must cast the variable *before* the division: `(double) y / x`[cite: 257].
list_items: **Implicit:** Java automatically upgrades an `int` to a `double` in mixed math[cite: 255].
**Explicit:** You manually force a conversion using `(dataType) variable`[cite: 257].

subtopics.title: Advanced Computation (Math.pow & Math.sqrt)
subtopics.content: Basic operators cannot handle advanced algebra like exponents or square roots. Java provides a built-in `Math` class filled with highly optimized scientific functions[cite: 269].
subtopics.callout_text: Almost all `Math` class methods (except for `abs`) require and return `double` floating-point numbers due to the high precision required for roots and logs[cite: 270].
list_items: `Math.pow(a, b)` evaluates $a^b$[cite: 270].
`Math.sqrt(a)` evaluates $\sqrt{a}$[cite: 270].

subtopics.title: Trigonometry & Radians
subtopics.content: The `Math` class also contains a full suite of trigonometric functions used for game physics and rotational math[cite: 269].
subtopics.callout_text: If you try to run `Math.sin(90)`, Java will calculate the sine of 90 radians, which is mathematically incorrect for what you want! Always use `Math.toRadians()` first[cite: 269]!
list_items: `Math.toRadians(a)` converts degrees to radians[cite: 269].
`Math.toDegrees(a)` converts radians back to degrees[cite: 269].
